en.extract:
The white-throated mountaingem or white-throated mountain-gem (Lampornis castaneoventris) is a species of hummingbird in tribe Lampornithini of subfamily Trochilinae. It is endemic to the highlands of Costa Rica and Panama.


Taxonomy
The white-throated mountaingem was formally described in 1851 by the English ornithologist John Gould based on a specimen collected by the Polish botanist Józef Warszewicz at an altidude of 1,800 m (6,000 ft) in the Cordillera de Chiriquí of western Panama. Gould coined the binomial name Trichilus castaneoventris where the specific epithet combines the Latin castaneus meaning "chestnut-brown" with venter, ventris meaning "belly". The white-throated mountaingem is now one of seven species placed in the genus Lampornis that was introduced in 1827 by the English zoologist William Swainson.
Two subspecies are recognised:

L. c. cinereicauda (Lawrence, GN, 1867) – southern Costa Rica (Cordillera de Talamanca)
L. c. castaneoventris (Gould, J, 1851) – montane forest of far western Panama
The subspecies L. c. cinereicauda has sometimes been considered a separate species, the gray-tailed mountaingem. In 1999 Karl-Ludwig Schuchmann in the Handbook of Birds of the World treated both the purple-throated mountaingem and the gray-tailed mountaingem as conspecific with the white-throated mountaingem, and used the English name, "Variable mountain-gem". Molecular genetic analyses using both mitochondrial and nuclear DNA sequences found that L. c. cinereicauda, L. c. castaneoventris and the purple-throated mountaingem (Lampornis calolaemus) formed a shallow unresolved clade.


Description

The grey-tailed mountaingem is about 12 cm (4.7 in) long and weighs about 5 to 6 g (0.18 to 0.21 oz). It has a medium-length straight black bill, dark cheeks, and a white stripe behind the eye. Males have mostly dark bronzy green upperparts with an emerald green crown and a black to bluish black tail. Their chin and throat are white, the sides of the neck and upper breast bright green, and the lower breast and vent area dark gray. Females have entirely bright green upperparts. Their central tail feathers are dark metallic to bronze green and the outer ones paler. Their throat and belly are dark rufous and the undertail coverts are gray with white or buff edges.


Distribution and habitat
The white-throated mountaingem is found only in the mountains of western Panama's Chiriquí Province and south-central Costa Rica. It inhabits the interior, edges, and shrubby clearings of oak forest and also gardens in communities near the forest. In elevation it ranges from 1,500 m (4,900 ft) up to timberline.


Behavior


Movement
The white-throated mountaingem is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The white-throated mountaingem feeds on nectar from a variety of flowering plants. Males typically feed at epiphytes in the forest interior while females more often feed in shrubby areas. Males are territorial, defending flower patches. They are dominant over smaller hummingbirds and subordinate to larger ones like the fiery-throated hummingbird (Panterpe insignis). The species also feeds on small arthropods gleaned from foliage.


Breeding
The white-throated mountaingem's breeding season spans from October to April. Its nest is a cup of fine fibers with moss and some lichen on the outside. The incubation length and time to fledging are not known.


Vocalization
The white-throated mountaingem makes high pitched calls described as "ziit or ziip" and also "a 'sputtery, bubbly' song".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the white-throated mountaingem as being of Least Concern, though it has a small range and its population size and trend are unknown. It is considered common. However, "this hummingbird is potentially threatened by human activities" such as deforestation for timber and agriculture.


References


Further reading
Stiles, F. Gary & Skutch, Alexander F. (1990): A guide to the birds of Costa Rica. Cornell University Press. ISBN 0-8014-9600-4

The '''white-throated mountaingem''' or '''white-throated mountain-gem''' ('''''Lampornis castaneoventris''''') is a species of [[hummingbird]] in tribe [[Lampornithini]] of subfamily [[Trochilinae]]. It is [[endemic]] to the highlands of [[Costa Rica]] and [[Panama]].

==Taxonomy==
The white-throated mountaingem was [[species description|formally described]] in 1851 by the English ornithologist [[John Gould]] based on a specimen collected by the Polish botanist [[Józef Warszewicz]] at an altidude of {{cvt|6000|ft|order=flip}} in the [[Chiriquí Province|Cordillera de Chiriquí]] of western Panama. Gould coined the [[binomial name]] ''Trichilus castaneoventris'' where the specific epithet combines the [[Latin]] ''castaneus'' meaning "chestnut-brown" with ''venter'', ''ventris'' meaning "belly".<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1850 | publication-date=1851 | title=On six new species of humming birds | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=18 | issue=211 | pages=162-164 [163] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12858873 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1945 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=5 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=83 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480094 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=castaneoventris | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=castaneoventris | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=2 April 2026 }}</ref> The white-throated mountaingem is now one of seven species placed in the genus ''[[Lampornis]]'' that was introduced in 1827 by the English zoologist [[William Swainson]].<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=2 April 2026 }}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist/>
* ''L. c. cinereicauda'' ([[George Newbold Lawrence|Lawrence, GN]], 1867) – southern Costa Rica ([[Cordillera de Talamanca]])
* ''L. c. castaneoventris'' ([[John Gould|Gould, J]], 1851) – montane forest of far western Panama

The subspecies ''L. c. cinereicauda'' has sometimes been considered a separate species, the gray-tailed mountaingem.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Hummingbirds | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/hummingbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=2 April 2026 }}</ref> In 1999 Karl-Ludwig Schuchmann in the ''[[Handbook of Birds of the World]]'' treated both the [[purple-throated mountaingem]] and the gray-tailed mountaingem as conspecific with the white-throated mountaingem, and used the English name, "Variable mountain-gem".<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Schuchmann | first=K.L. | year=1999 | chapter=Family Trochilidae (Hummingbirds) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=5: Barn-owls to Hummingbirds | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-87334-25-2 | pages=468-680 | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0005unse/page/468/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref> Molecular genetic analyses using both mitochondrial and nuclear DNA sequences found that ''L. c. cinereicauda'', ''L. c. castaneoventris'' and the [[purple-throated mountaingem]] (''Lampornis calolaemus'') formed a shallow unresolved clade.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=García-Moreno | first1=J. | last2=Cortés | first2=N. | last3=García-Deras | first3=G.M. | last4=Hernández-Baños | first4=B.E. | date=2006 | title=Local origin and diversification among ''Lampornis'' hummingbirds: A Mesoamerican taxon | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=38 | issue=2 | pages=488-498 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2005.08.015}}</ref><ref name=mcguire2014>{{ cite journal | last1=McGuire | first1=J. | last2=Witt | first2=C. | last3=Remsen | first3=J.V. | last4=Corl | first4=A. | last5=Rabosky | first5=D. | last6=Altshuler | first6=D. | last7=Dudley | first7=R. | date=2014 | title=Molecular phylogenetics and the diversification of hummingbirds | journal=Current Biology | volume=24 | issue=8 | pages=910-916 | doi=10.1016/j.cub.2014.03.016 | doi-access=free }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:065 Male White-throated mountaingem in Los Quetzales National Park Photo by Giles Laurent.jpg|alt=Male|thumb|Male]]
[[File:066 Female White-throated mountaingem in Los Quetzales National Park Photo by Giles Laurent.jpg|alt=Female|thumb|Female]]
The grey-tailed mountaingem is about {{convert|12|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|5|to|6|g|oz|abbr=on}}. It has a medium-length straight black bill, dark cheeks, and a white stripe behind the eye. Males have mostly dark bronzy green upperparts with an emerald green crown and a black to bluish black tail. Their chin and throat are white, the sides of the neck and upper breast bright green, and the lower breast and vent area dark gray. Females have entirely bright green upperparts. Their central tail feathers are dark metallic to bronze green and the outer ones paler. Their throat and belly are dark rufous and the undertail [[Covert feather|coverts]] are gray with white or buff edges.<ref name=WTMG-BOW>{{cite web | last=Juárez | first=R. | date=2024 | title=White-throated Mountain-gem (''Lampornis castaneoventris''), version 2.0 | editor1-last=Kirwan | editor1-first=G.M. | editor-last2=Schulenberg | editor2-first=T.S. | work=Birds of the World | location=Ithaca, NY, USA | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | url=https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.wtmgem1.02  | access-date=3 April 2026 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The white-throated mountaingem is found only in the mountains of western Panama's [[Chiriquí Province]] and south-central Costa Rica. It inhabits the interior, edges, and shrubby clearings of oak forest and also gardens in communities near the forest. In elevation it ranges from {{convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}} up to [[Tree line|timberline]].<ref name=WTMG-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The white-throated mountaingem is a year-round resident.<ref name=WTMG-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The white-throated mountaingem feeds on nectar from a variety of flowering plants. Males typically feed at [[epiphyte]]s in the forest interior while females more often feed in shrubby areas. Males are territorial, defending flower patches. They are dominant over smaller hummingbirds and subordinate to larger ones like the [[fiery-throated hummingbird]] (''Panterpe insignis''). The species also feeds on small [[arthropod]]s gleaned from foliage.<ref name=WTMG-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The white-throated mountaingem's breeding season spans from October to April. Its nest is a cup of fine fibers with moss and some lichen on the outside. The incubation length and time to fledging are not known.<ref name=WTMG-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Lampornis-castaneoventris |species=white-throated mountaingem}}
===Vocalization===

The white-throated mountaingem makes high pitched calls described as "''ziit'' or ''ziip''" and also "a 'sputtery, bubbly' song".<ref name=WTMG-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the white-throated mountaingem as being of [[Least Concern]], though it has a small range and its population size and trend are unknown.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered common. However, "this hummingbird is potentially threatened by human activities" such as deforestation for timber and agriculture.<ref name=WTMG-BOW/>

==References==
{{Reflist}}

==Further reading==
* Stiles, F. Gary & Skutch, Alexander F. (1990): ''A guide to the birds of Costa Rica''. Cornell University Press. {{ISBN|0-8014-9600-4}}

zh.extract:
白喉宝石蜂鸟（学名：Lampornis castaneoventris），是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
白喉宝石蜂鸟体重约5.08克，翼长约61毫米，嘴峰长约23.6毫米，喙宽度约2.2毫米，喙厚度约2.4毫米，跗蹠长约5.9毫米，尾长约34.8毫米。白喉宝石蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。


亚种
L. c. castaneoventris：分布于巴拿马极西部的山地森林。
L. c. cinereicauda：分布于哥斯达黎加南部的塔拉曼卡山脉，有时也被视为单独的物种灰尾宝石蜂鸟。
